option_a: Cutting down trees to plant new ones
option_b: Watchtowers to spot forest fires
option_c: Fenced areas to round up wild reindeer
option_d: Land boundary markers from old government surveys
option_e: Oil & gas well pads (in-situ extraction)
explanation_text: These tiny identical clearings arranged in a perfect grid are oil and gas well pads used for in-situ bitumen extraction (SAGD method). Steam is injected deep underground to liquefy the bitumen and pump it to the surface — no open-pit mining required. The access roads and pipelines connecting each pad create the distinctive grid pattern visible from space.

option_a: Large fuel storage tanks for an oil refinery
option_b: Water treatment and purification facility
option_c: Sports stadium complex under construction
option_d: Aircraft hangar base for military jets
option_e: Nuclear power plant reactor containment buildings
explanation_text: The two large circular domes are reactor containment buildings — thick reinforced concrete shells designed to prevent radioactive material from escaping in case of an accident. Cattenom nuclear power plant in northeastern France operates 4 reactors and supplies about 8% of France's total electricity production.

option_a: Thousands of greenhouses growing vegetables
option_b: Military camouflage netting covering a base
option_c: Large fish farming pools laid out in rows
option_d: Rows of crops irrigated by drip farming
option_e: Giant solar panel farm in the desert
explanation_text: Bhadla Solar Park in Rajasthan, India is one of the largest solar farms in the world, covering over 56 km². The uniform blue-grey grid you see is made up of millions of photovoltaic panels absorbing sunlight in one of the hottest and sunniest deserts on Earth. The small building visible in the corner is a grid substation connecting the electricity to the national power network.

option_a: Craters left by ancient meteorite impacts
option_b: Giant salt lakes drying up in the desert
option_c: Oil storage tanks seen from very high altitude
option_d: Military bombing test zones in a restricted area
option_e: Crops grown in the desert using rotating water sprinklers
explanation_text: These perfect green circles in the middle of the Sahara Desert are wheat and potato fields irrigated by centre-pivot systems — giant rotating sprinkler arms up to 400 meters long that water crops in a perfect circle. The water is pumped from the Nubian Sandstone Aquifer, a prehistoric underground reservoir containing water that fell as rain over 10,000 years ago.

option_a: Rows of solar panels floating on a reservoir
option_b: Fish farming nets laid out in a coastal bay
option_c: Seaweed farming plots in a shallow sea
option_d: Flooded rice paddies seen during monsoon season
option_e: Floating vegetable gardens built on a lake
explanation_text: These remarkable strips are floating gardens on Inle Lake in Myanmar, built by the Intha people over 1000 years ago. Farmers weave together lake weeds, hyacinths and mud to create long rectangular islands anchored to the lake bed with bamboo poles. Tomatoes, beans and flowers are grown on these living rafts — which slowly drift and shift position over time.

option_a: Ancient ceremonial site with roads leading to a temple
option_b: Cracks in dry earth seen after an extreme drought
option_c: Runways of a large abandoned military airbase
option_d: A spider web pattern formed by natural river erosion
option_e: Farming plots radiating outward from a central village
explanation_text: These remarkable star-shaped patterns in the Bolivian Amazon were created by Mennonite and cooperative farming communities in the 1970s. Each family received a triangular plot of land radiating outward from a central village, giving everyone equal access to the road, the church and the market at the centre. Dozens of these 'wheels' cover the Santa Cruz region and are regularly photographed by NASA satellites.

option_a: A volcanic crater lake inside an old eruption cone
option_b: A large water reservoir built to supply a city
option_c: A sinkhole that swallowed part of a neighbourhood
option_d: An outdoor amphitheatre carved into a hillside
option_e: A giant open-pit mine dug in the middle of a city
explanation_text: The Mirny Diamond Mine in Siberia, Russia is one of the largest excavated holes on Earth — 1200 meters wide and 525 meters deep. The turquoise water at the bottom is groundwater that flooded the pit after mining stopped in 2001. Between 1957 and 2001 it produced over 200 million carats of diamonds. The spiral roads carved into the walls were used by trucks taking 2 hours to drive from the bottom to the surface.

option_a: Cargo ships that ran aground during a storm
option_b: A military naval base with vessels under repair
option_c: An offshore oil platform being towed to shore
option_d: A ferry terminal with boats waiting to dock
option_e: Giant ships deliberately beached to be dismantled for scrap metal
explanation_text: The Chittagong ship breaking yard in Bangladesh is one of the largest ship dismantling sites in the world. At high tide, end-of-life supertankers and cargo ships are deliberately driven at full speed onto the beach. At low tide, 200,000 workers dismantle them piece by piece using basic tools. The spherical tanks visible on the left ship identify it as a liquefied gas carrier. This single beach recycles around 20% of the world's retired shipping tonnage.

option_a: A highway interchange with complex on and off ramps
option_b: A theme park with roller coasters along the waterfront
option_c: A bus and tram depot with maintenance tracks
option_d: A temporary market and event space along a river
option_e: A Formula 1 street circuit winding through a city centre
explanation_text: The Singapore Grand Prix uses public roads around the Marina Bay waterfront as a Formula 1 race track. Unlike permanent circuits, the entire track is assembled and dismantled every year using temporary barriers, grandstands and lighting rigs. The pit lane is visible along the waterfront. Singapore hosts the only F1 night race in the world, requiring over 1500 lighting fixtures to illuminate the circuit.

option_a: A busy international airport seen during a major travel strike
option_b: A military airbase with planes ready for deployment
option_c: A commercial aircraft factory with newly built planes outside
option_d: An air show preparation site with planes parked before display
option_e: Thousands of retired military aircraft stored in the desert
explanation_text: The Aircraft Maintenance and Regeneration Group (AMARG) at Davis-Monthan Air Force Base in Tucson, Arizona is the largest aircraft storage and preservation facility in the world. Over 4000 retired US military aircraft — including B-52 bombers, F-14 fighters, C-5 Galaxy transports and A-10 Warthogs — are parked in rows grouped by type. The dry desert air preserves the aluminium airframes perfectly. Some aircraft are kept in flyable condition and can be reactivated within days if needed.

location_name: Naval Weapons Station Yorktown Ammunition Depot
option_a: A botanical garden with hedged plant collection sections
option_b: A water treatment plant with filtration beds
option_c: A prison complex with exercise yards between buildings
option_d: A research greenhouse facility divided into test plots
option_e: Underground military ammunition bunkers hidden under grass mounds
explanation_text: These grass-covered rectangular mounds are ammunition igloos — reinforced concrete bunkers built just below ground level and covered with soil and vegetation for camouflage and blast containment. Located at Naval Weapons Station Yorktown in Virginia, USA, each igloo stores naval munitions, torpedoes or missiles. The thick earth covering serves two purposes: it hides the facility from aerial surveillance and contains any accidental explosion. The small white doors visible at each end are the only clue from above.

option_a: Outdoor swimming pools at a large desert holiday resort
option_b: Fish and shrimp farming ponds in an arid region
option_c: Coloured salt pans harvested for cooking and cosmetics
option_d: Cooling ponds for a nuclear power plant in the desert
option_e: Evaporation pools used to extract lithium from underground brine
explanation_text: These vivid turquoise and green rectangles in Chile's Atacama Desert are lithium evaporation pools. Brine rich in lithium is pumped from underground aquifers and left to evaporate in the sun for 12 to 18 months. The colour of each pool reveals its stage — bright turquoise means fresh brine, deep green means the lithium concentration is increasing, white means the salt has fully crystallised. Chile holds the world's largest lithium reserves and supplies the raw material for electric vehicle batteries used by Tesla, Samsung and Apple.

option_a: A densely packed refugee camp in the Middle East
option_b: A ancient mud-brick city abandoned in the desert
option_c: A sprawling informal slum on the outskirts of a city
option_d: Archaeological ruins of a Roman trading settlement
option_e: The world's largest cemetery stretching across a desert city
explanation_text: Wadi-us-Salaam (Valley of Peace) in Najaf, Iraq is the largest cemetery in the world, covering over 6 km² and containing more than 6 million burials. From above it is almost indistinguishable from a living city — a dense maze of crypts, mausoleums and burial chambers in sand-coloured brick, with narrow paths running between them. It has been an active burial site for over 1,400 years and continues to grow by thousands of graves every year.

option_a: The real Eiffel Tower in Paris France
option_b: A radio transmission tower in a city park
option_c: A theme park ride shaped like a famous landmark
option_d: A construction crane over a circular building site
option_e: A replica of the Eiffel Tower built in a Chinese ghost town
explanation_text: This is not Paris — it is Tianducheng, a residential development near Hangzhou in China built in 2007 to look exactly like a French city, complete with a 108-metre replica of the Eiffel Tower and Haussmann-style boulevards. Built for 10,000 residents, it remained almost completely empty for years, earning it the nickname 'China's ghost Paris'. The circular garden and abandoned surroundings visible from satellite are the clearest clue that something is not quite right.

option_a: A car racing circuit for a desert grand prix
option_b: A runway extension for a small regional airport
option_c: A military vehicle testing track in an arid zone
option_d: A cycling velodrome built on the outskirts of a city
option_e: A camel racing track on the edge of a desert city
explanation_text: This elongated oval track on the edge of a desert city is a camel racing circuit — one of the most popular traditional sports in the Gulf region. The small dark dots visible on the sand inside and around the track are camels. Unlike horse racing tracks which are oval, camel tracks are much longer and narrower, sometimes stretching up to 10 km. The jockeys are now remote-controlled robots weighing 27 kg, operated by owners following the race in SUVs alongside the track.

option_a: A group of oil storage tanks inside a refinery
option_b: Giant parasols shading an outdoor market square
option_c: Satellite dishes grouped on a rooftop communications hub
option_d: Circular skylights on top of a large underground mall
option_e: A cluster of mushroom-shaped water towers in a city park
explanation_text: These circular shapes seen from above are the tops of Kuwait's famous Mushroom Water Towers — distinctive elevated water reservoirs built in the 1970s across Kuwait City. Each tower sits on a single concrete stem and flares out into a wide circular cap, resembling a giant mushroom. They were designed by Swedish architect Sune Lindstrom and serve both as functional water storage and as urban landmarks. Five groups of these towers are scattered across the city, each painted in different colours.

option_a: Solar panels installed on the roof of a shopping mall
option_b: Greenhouse tunnels for growing vegetables in the desert
option_c: Aircraft hangars at a small military airbase
option_d: A water desalination plant with filtration membrane units
option_e: Shaded car parking structures protecting vehicles from the desert sun
explanation_text: These grid-like structures in Bahrain are not solar panels — they are shading canopies built over car parking lots to protect vehicles from the intense Gulf sun, where temperatures regularly exceed 45°C. The metal frames and their shadows create a pattern almost identical to solar panels from above. This type of covered parking is standard infrastructure across Gulf countries, where an unshaded car can reach 80°C inside within minutes.

option_a: Crude oil storage tanks at a petroleum terminal
option_b: Liquefied gas tanks at an industrial plant
option_c: Circular sports arenas seen from very high altitude
option_d: Sewage treatment settling tanks on the edge of a city
option_e: Giant drinking water storage tanks supplying a desert city
explanation_text: These three large circular structures in Bahrain are elevated drinking water storage tanks — critical infrastructure in a country with no natural rivers or lakes. Bahrain relies almost entirely on desalinated seawater, which is processed and then stored in these giant reinforced concrete tanks before being distributed across the city. The long shadows reveal how tall they are. The ribbed dome pattern on top is a structural reinforcement technique. They are easily confused with oil tanks, but the white dome finish and residential neighbourhood context are key clues.

location_name: Bahrain Durrat Al Bahrain Residential Islands
option_a: A coral reef ecosystem photographed from above
option_b: A natural lagoon archipelago formed by tidal erosion
option_c: An oil drilling platform complex in the Persian Gulf
option_d: A fish farming cooperative with netted sea enclosures
option_e: Artificial islands built in the sea to create luxury waterfront homes
explanation_text: These remarkable shapes in the Persian Gulf are Durrat Al Bahrain — a collection of entirely man-made islands constructed between 2004 and 2015. The islands are shaped like fish, horseshoes and crescent moons, each lined with luxury villas that all have direct sea access and private jetties — visible as tiny white spikes along the shoreline. Bahrain has been reclaiming land from the sea for decades and today over 80% of its northern coastline is artificial.

option_a: A drive-through fast food restaurant with a long queue
option_b: A toll booth plaza on a busy urban highway
option_c: A car wash facility with vehicles waiting in line
option_d: A bus depot with vehicles lined up for inspection
option_e: A petrol station with cars queuing to fill up
explanation_text: The yellow arrow points to the fuel pump canopy of a petrol station in Manama, Bahrain. The long queue of vehicles stretching back onto the main road is a common sight in Gulf countries where fuel is heavily subsidised by the government — petrol in Bahrain costs less than bottled water. The rectangular canopy shading the pumps, the wide forecourt and the curved entry and exit lanes are the key clues visible from satellite.

option_a: A government administration building in a residential block
option_b: A primary school with a courtyard in the centre
option_c: A police or fire station serving the local neighbourhood
option_d: A community health clinic with a small garden
option_e: A small mosque slightly rotated to face the direction of Mecca
explanation_text: The yellow arrow points to a small neighbourhood mosque in Abu Dhabi, UAE. The key clue is the orientation — unlike every surrounding building which follows the street grid, the mosque is rotated to align with the Qibla, the direction of Mecca in Saudi Arabia. This slight angular offset is visible from satellite and is one of the most reliable ways to identify a mosque in a Middle Eastern city from above. The small dome on the roof confirms the identification.

option_a: An athletics stadium with a football pitch in the centre
option_b: A construction site surrounded by a temporary access road
option_c: A horse show jumping arena next to a racetrack
option_d: An abandoned fairground inside a recreational park
option_e: A military or police obstacle training course inside a running track
explanation_text: The yellow rectangle highlights a military or police physical training facility in Abu Dhabi, UAE. The oval pink track surrounding it is a standard running track, but the interior contains scattered obstacles, low walls, climbing frames and crawling areas typical of military fitness training. The random placement of the obstacles — unlike the neat lines of a sports field — and the absence of grass or markings are the key clues from above. Facilities like this are common on Gulf military bases.

location_name: Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum Solar Tower (CSP)
option_a: A giant flat solar panel farm seen from very high altitude
option_b: A radar installation with thousands of antenna receivers
option_c: A circular airport with runways radiating from a central hub
option_d: A satellite ground control station with tracking dishes
option_e: A concentrated solar power plant using mirrors to focus sunlight onto a central tower
explanation_text: This is a Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) tower plant in the Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum Solar Park in Dubai, UAE. Unlike standard photovoltaic panels which convert sunlight directly to electricity, CSP plants use thousands of motorised mirrors called heliostats — each individually angled to reflect sunlight onto a single receiver at the top of the central tower. The intense heat (over 500°C) generates steam to drive a turbine. The bright glowing spot at the base of the tower is the focused reflected light from all mirrors combined — visible even from satellite.

option_a: A nature reserve with lakes and jungle protected from farming
option_b: A flooded river delta seen during monsoon season
option_c: A botanical garden and arboretum on the edge of a city
option_d: An irrigated rice paddy complex with drainage canals
option_e: A golf course standing out as a green oasis in a dry landscape
explanation_text: This vivid green rectangle in the middle of a dry Cambodian landscape is a golf course. The contrast with the surrounding brown scrubland is immediate — maintaining a golf course in a tropical dry season requires enormous amounts of water. The sinuous fairways, sand bunkers (the beige kidney shapes) and dark blue water hazards are the classic signatures of a golf course from above. The red-roofed clubhouse is visible at the bottom centre.

option_a: An open-pit mine seen from above
option_b: A giant sand dune in the desert
option_c: A bulk material storage facility
option_d: A construction site being levelled
option_e: A sacred Aboriginal rock formation rising from the desert
explanation_text: Uluru, also known as Ayers Rock, is a massive sandstone monolith rising 348 metres above the flat desert of the Northern Territory, Australia. Sacred to the Anangu Aboriginal people for tens of thousands of years, it is one of Australia's most iconic landmarks. From satellite its deep rust-red colour, smooth rounded top and dramatic shadow instantly distinguish it from the surrounding flat desert. The area around its base shows the tourist infrastructure — car parks, walking paths and the cultural centre.

option_a: A horse racing track in the desert
option_b: A private airstrip for light aircraft
option_c: A research facility in a restricted zone
option_d: A car racing circuit for desert motorsport
option_e: A camel racing track in the UAE desert
explanation_text: This elongated oval track in the UAE is a camel racing circuit. Camel racing is one of the oldest and most cherished traditional sports in the Arabian Peninsula. The track is much longer and narrower than a horse racing oval — some stretching up to 10 km. Today the jockeys are remote-controlled robots operated by owners who follow the race in SUVs alongside the track. The sandy terrain inside the oval and the absence of any grandstand infrastructure are key clues from satellite.

location_name: Open-Air Cinema in the Egyptian Desert
option_a: An artistic land art installation
option_b: Solar panels arranged in a pattern
option_c: A natural rock formation
option_d: An ancient Roman theatre
option_e: An open-air cinema carved into the desert rock
explanation_text: This remarkable site in the Sinai Desert, Egypt is an open-air cinema — rows of seats carved directly into the rocky hillside facing a flat screen wall. From satellite, the curved rows of seats and the rectangular screen structure are barely distinguishable from natural rock formations, making this one of the most surprising identifications possible. Open-air cinemas are common in resort areas of the Red Sea coast where the dry desert climate makes outdoor screenings possible year-round.

location_name: Mosque in the Sinai Desert Egypt
option_a: A shopping mall with a central atrium
option_b: A research centre in a restricted area
option_c: An industrial factory complex
option_d: A museum with a sculpted roof
option_e: A mosque with a distinctive dome and courtyard
explanation_text: This building in the Sinai Peninsula, Egypt is a mosque. The key identifying features visible from satellite are the rectangular prayer hall, the central dome, the enclosed courtyard for ablutions before prayer, and the orientation slightly offset from the road grid — aligned toward Mecca. Unlike churches or temples, mosques rarely have parking lots or gardens in front. The clean geometric layout and the dome are the most reliable clues from above.

option_a: An abandoned construction site
option_b: An artistic land installation
option_c: A paintball and outdoor gaming facility
option_d: A children's adventure playground
option_e: A surface-to-air missile battery installation
explanation_text: These geometric earthworks in the Egyptian desert are a SAM (Surface-to-Air Missile) battery — a military air defence installation. The circular revetments (protective earth berms) arranged in a star or flower pattern are designed to shelter missile launchers while allowing them to rotate and fire in any direction. The pattern is a Cold War-era Soviet design widely exported to Middle Eastern countries. From above, the symmetrical arrangement of mounds and the cleared central area are the distinctive clues.

option_a: A dense urban neighbourhood
option_b: An open-pit mining operation
option_c: A refugee camp in a desert region
option_d: A desert cemetery with individual plots
option_e: A solid waste treatment and landfill facility
explanation_text: This site in the Sinai Peninsula, Egypt is a waste treatment and landfill facility. From above, the irregular plots separated by access tracks, the varying colours of decomposing waste, and the absence of any regular street grid distinguish it from a residential area or cemetery — though the similarity is striking. Waste management infrastructure in desert regions often appears surprisingly organic and irregular from satellite, making it one of the hardest site types to identify correctly.

option_a: A covered car parking structure
option_b: A landfill site on the edge of a town
option_c: A shopping centre under construction
option_d: A shrimp and fish aquaculture farm
option_e: A wastewater treatment plant with circular settling tanks
explanation_text: These circular structures in the Sinai Peninsula are wastewater treatment settling tanks — large circular basins where sewage is slowly rotated to separate solids from liquids. The distinctive round shapes with a central pivot arm are the global standard for this type of infrastructure. From satellite they can be confused with fish farms or covered parking, but the arrangement in pairs or groups next to rectangular processing buildings is the key clue.

option_a: Agricultural planting rows in a field
option_b: Building foundations of an ancient city
option_c: Natural erosion lines in rocky terrain
option_d: An archaeological excavation site
option_e: Ancient giant drawings etched into the desert plateau
explanation_text: The Nazca Lines in southern Peru are enormous geoglyphs — drawings of animals, plants and geometric shapes scraped into the reddish desert surface by removing the top layer of pebbles to reveal the lighter ground beneath. Created by the Nazca culture between 500 BCE and 500 CE, they cover over 450 km². They are only fully visible from the air — from the ground they are nearly impossible to make out. The lines are so precise and large that they were only discovered by aviation in the 1920s.

option_a: A reservoir behind a hydroelectric dam
option_b: A digitally altered or edited image
option_c: An abandoned open-pit mine flooded with water
option_d: A natural freshwater lake
option_e: A volcano inside a lake inside a volcano
explanation_text: Taal Volcano in the Philippines is one of the most unusual geological formations on Earth — a volcanic island sitting inside a crater lake (Lake Taal), which itself sits inside a larger ancient volcanic caldera. From satellite the concentric rings of water and land create a striking bull's-eye pattern. Taal is one of the most active volcanoes in the Philippines and has erupted over 30 times in recorded history. Its last major eruption was in January 2020.

option_a: A lime and calcium production plant
option_b: A cement manufacturing facility
option_c: A grain milling and processing plant
option_d: A copper smelting and refining plant
option_e: An aluminium smelting plant
explanation_text: This large industrial complex in Bahrain is an aluminium smelter — one of the largest in the world. Aluminium Bahrain (Alba) produces over 1.5 million tonnes of aluminium per year. The distinctive features visible from satellite are the long rectangular pot lines (electrolysis cells where aluminium is extracted from alumina), the large carbon plant buildings, and the port facility for importing raw materials. Aluminium smelting requires enormous amounts of electricity — the plant has its own dedicated power station.

option_a: A munitions and explosives production facility
option_b: A chemical processing plant
option_c: A sewage and wastewater treatment plant
option_d: A heavy oil upgrading refinery
option_e: A seawater desalination plant producing drinking water
explanation_text: This industrial complex in Bahrain is a desalination plant — a facility that removes salt from seawater to produce drinking water. Bahrain has no natural freshwater sources and relies almost entirely on desalination to supply its population. The key features visible from satellite are the long parallel rows of reverse osmosis membrane units, the seawater intake channels, and the large storage tanks for the treated water. Bahrain was one of the first countries in the world to adopt large-scale desalination.

option_a: Grain silos and cereal storage facility
option_b: Underground munitions storage bunkers
option_c: Crude oil storage tanks at a refinery
option_d: Elevated water towers serving a city
option_e: Liquefied natural gas (LNG) storage tanks
explanation_text: These enormous spherical and cylindrical tanks in Qatar are LNG (Liquefied Natural Gas) storage tanks. Qatar is the world's largest LNG exporter, and these cryogenic tanks store natural gas cooled to -162°C until it is loaded onto tanker ships. The distinctive double-walled insulated tanks — some over 80 metres in diameter — are immediately recognisable from satellite by their perfect circular shape and the frost patterns visible around their bases. Qatar's North Field is the world's largest single natural gas reservoir.

option_a: Grain silos on a farm
option_b: Underground ammunition bunkers
option_c: Crude oil storage at a refinery
option_d: Liquefied gas storage tanks
option_e: Large drinking water storage reservoirs
explanation_text: These circular tanks in Bahrain are large elevated water storage reservoirs — essential infrastructure in a country with no natural freshwater sources. Unlike oil tanks (which are usually dark grey or black with floating roofs) or gas tanks (which are spherical), water reservoirs in the Gulf are typically white or light-coloured reinforced concrete cylinders with domed roofs. Their location next to a residential area and the absence of pipework typical of petrochemical facilities are the key distinguishing clues.

option_a: A scientific research campus
option_b: A warehouse and logistics park
option_c: An automobile assembly plant
option_d: A residential housing complex
option_e: A gas-fired electricity generating power plant
explanation_text: This large industrial complex in Bahrain is a gas-fired power plant — a facility that burns natural gas to generate electricity. The distinctive features visible from satellite are the large rectangular turbine halls, the tall exhaust stacks, the cooling water infrastructure along the coastline, and the high-voltage transmission lines radiating outward from the substation. Gulf countries rely heavily on gas-fired power plants given their abundant natural gas reserves and extreme electricity demand for air conditioning.

option_a: A luxury coastal hotel complex
option_b: A large shopping and entertainment mall
option_c: A technology research and development campus
option_d: A nuclear power plant with reactor domes
option_e: A gas-fired power plant on the coast
explanation_text: This large industrial facility on the coast of Taiwan is a gas-fired power plant. Despite the similarity with a nuclear plant (large buildings, coastal location for cooling water intake), the absence of reactor containment domes and the presence of tall exhaust stacks and LNG storage tanks nearby are the distinguishing features. Taiwan has been gradually replacing its nuclear capacity with gas-fired generation as part of its energy transition policy.

option_a: A marine mammal aquarium park
option_b: A fish and shellfish aquaculture farm
option_c: A marina with boats stored on land
option_d: A water theme park and resort
option_e: A rotating boat lift connecting two canals at different heights
explanation_text: The Falkirk Wheel in Scotland is the world's only rotating boat lift — a unique engineering structure that lifts boats 24 metres between the Forth and Clyde Canal and the Union Canal. Two gondolas filled with water rotate 180 degrees like a giant wheel, using barely more electricity than boiling a kettle thanks to Archimedes' principle. From satellite the circular rotating arm and the two canal basins at different levels create a distinctive pattern unlike any other infrastructure.

option_a: Ancient ruins of a medieval structure
option_b: A natural rock formation
option_c: A mountain bike trail carved into a hillside
option_d: Bales of straw arranged in a field
option_e: A giant train sculpture made entirely from bricks
explanation_text: This extraordinary site in County Durham, England is a full-scale replica of a Victorian steam train made entirely from locally sourced red brick — a land art installation called Brick Train by artist David Mach, created in 1997. The train appears to be emerging from a hillside at full speed. From satellite the brick-red colour and the unmistakable silhouette of a locomotive with carriages make it one of the most surprising satellite discoveries in the UK.

option_a: A scientific research facility
option_b: A full-scale aircraft flight simulator
option_c: A Hollywood film set for an aviation movie
option_d: An aviation museum with outdoor exhibits
option_e: A giant hangar used to store and maintain large aircraft
explanation_text: This enormous circular building in Ocala, Florida is one of the largest aircraft hangars in the world — originally built to house US Navy blimps (airships) during World War II. The structure is so large that clouds can form inside when the humidity is high enough. Today it is used to store and maintain aircraft. From satellite the perfect circular footprint and the vast scale relative to the surrounding buildings make it immediately striking — no other building type has this combination of size and circular shape.
